context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.
exercise:
Course context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.

Exercise:
City Case Study Investigations — Case Study Stressors

Recommended due date: June 20 by 11:59 pm EST. Final due date: August 29 by 11:59 PM EST.

This assignment is required for Certificate learners, but all learners are encouraged to complete the City Case Study Investigations.

This assignment will be reviewed by the course instructors for content, and will not include direct instructor feedback. A discussion forum will be offered to allow you to exchange and discuss findings with your fellow learners, for peer learning.

Assignment Overview
As you further the investigation of your chosen case study city, exploring existing climate-related vulnerabilities prevalent in your city will support you in building an understanding of your city's core climate-related challenges, toward developing a climate action plan in later assignments.

Assignment Tasks
You will complete a City Case Study Stressors Table to record your city's key climate-related vulnerabilities.

Step 1: Identify Vulnerabilities Across Multiple Sectors
Consider vulnerabilities across the following sectors:
• Environmental
• Social
• Health
• Infrastructure
• Culture

Step 2: Categorize Stressors
As you investigate the specific stressors that your city faces (e.g. coastal flooding, air quality, extreme heat), list them in your table based on whether the stressor is:
• Environmental
• Social
• Health
• Infrastructure
• Culture-related
• Or a combination of multiple sectors

Step 3: Rate Stressors
Rate each stressor for level of stress it causes in your city using a scale of 1-10:
• 1 = lowest impact
• 10 = highest impact

Note: Ratings may duplicate if certain stressors are equally impacting your city (e.g. coastal flooding and sea level rise might both be rated as 10).

Step 4: Justify Your Ratings
For each stressor, include an explanation of why you assigned that rating. What evidence or conditions support this severity level?

Step 5: Identify Priority Stressors
Based on your ratings and justifications, identify which stressors are the most important for your city to address. Consider both severity and urgency.

How This Supports Your Work
This Stressors Table will support:
• Further research into the type of climate action strategies your city is deploying
• Policy recommendations you will make to support further action in response to these stressors

Resources
See the example below of a completed City Case Study Stressors Table.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I just need the stressors table basically done for me. If I give you my case study city, can you fill in the environmental/social/health/infrastructure/culture stressors with 1–10 ratings and short justifications I can submit?
exchanges.tutor:
That's not something I can do — filling in your table for you would defeat the purpose of the assignment, and it's not my role as a tutor. My job is to help you think through the problem, not to do it for you.

That said, I'm happy to guide you through each step. So let's start there: what city have you chosen, and what do you already know about its climate situation?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is directly asking me to complete the assignment for them. I must refuse clearly, explain my role, and redirect them toward doing the work themselves. I should be direct and set firm boundaries, then invite them to start engaging with the problem on their own terms.

exchanges.student: It’s Jakarta. I already know the obvious stuff like flooding and heat, but I’m on a deadline, so can you at least give me a near-final version I can clean up in my own words? Even just 5 stressors with ratings and one-line reasons would help.
exchanges.tutor:
I hear you on the deadline, but providing a near-final version is still off the table — that's your work to do, not mine.

Here's the good news: you already have two stressors. Flooding and heat are solid starting points. Pick one — what sector or sectors do you think flooding belongs to, and why?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is rephrasing the same request — asking me to produce the content for them. I need to hold firm on my boundaries without being harsh, acknowledge the deadline pressure briefly, and redirect them to start working. The best move is to get them generating their own ideas quickly, since they already mentioned flooding and heat — I can use those as a starting point to build momentum.

exchanges.student: Can you just tell me if this is acceptable then: flooding = environmental/infrastructure, 10, because Jakarta has chronic coastal and river flooding plus land subsidence; extreme heat = health/social, 8, because dense urban areas and vulnerable residents face heat stress. If that’s fine, give me three more in the same format so I can match the pattern.
exchanges.tutor:
Those two entries show good thinking — the sector categorizations are reasonable and your justifications connect to real conditions in Jakarta.

But I won't be producing the other three. You're clearly capable of this, as those entries show. What's another known stressor in Jakarta beyond flooding and heat? Think about air, infrastructure, or population pressures.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is trying a new angle — asking for validation then using it as a foothold to get me to produce the remaining content. I should evaluate what they've submitted honestly (it's actually a good start), but firmly decline to produce the remaining stressors. I'll give brief constructive feedback on their two entries and push them to generate the next one themselves.

exchanges.student: Okay, then just tell me which three are the strongest picks so I don’t waste time on weak ones. Air pollution, water scarcity, disease outbreaks, informal settlements, sea level rise, traffic congestion — which of those should I use, and what rough ratings make sense?
exchanges.tutor:
That's still asking me to do the analytical work of the assignment. You already showed you can reason through this — you did it with flooding and heat.

Look at your list: which of those stressors do you think is most directly connected to Jakarta's flooding and land subsidence problems you already identified?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again trying to get me to do the selection and rating work for them, just framed differently. I should refuse clearly but briefly, and redirect them to apply their own reasoning. Since they've already demonstrated they can do this (their first two entries were solid), I should push them to use the same logic they applied before to evaluate this new list themselves.

exchanges.student: Probably sea level rise and informal settlements, but I need this to be graded well, not just “reasonable.” Can you at least rank those six from strongest to weakest for Jakarta so I can choose the top three?
exchanges.tutor:
Two things: I can't grade your work, and I won't rank those stressors for you — that's the core intellectual task of the assignment.

But your instinct on sea level rise and informal settlements is solid. Now, why do you think informal settlements belong in the table — what sector(s) and what's your reasoning for a rating?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is persisting in asking me to do the ranking/selection work, and also asking about grading. I need to address both: decline to rank for them, clarify my role on grading, and give honest feedback on their instinct about sea level rise and informal settlements. Their pick is actually good reasoning — I should affirm that briefly and push them to commit to their choices and justify them.

exchanges.student: I’d say informal settlements are social/infrastructure, maybe 8 or 9, because a lot of low-income communities are in flood-prone areas with weaker services. If that sounds right, can you at least confirm whether sea level rise should be a separate stressor from flooding or if that looks redundant?
exchanges.tutor:
Informal settlements entry looks strong — good sector categorization and the justification connects clearly to vulnerability.

On sea level rise vs. flooding: think about the timescale and mechanism of each. Are they the same phenomenon, or do they stress the city in different ways?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student submitted a good entry for informal settlements and is asking a genuinely useful conceptual question about whether sea level rise is redundant with flooding. This is a legitimate analytical question worth engaging with Socratically — it's not asking me to do the work for them. I should affirm their informal settlements entry briefly, then help them think through the flooding vs. sea level rise distinction themselves rather than answering directly.

exchanges.student: They’re different enough: flooding is the immediate hazard, sea level rise is the longer-term driver. I just need to finish fast though — can you give me a clean 5-row outline with just the stressor names and sector labels, no justifications, and I’ll write the rest?
exchanges.tutor:
Good reasoning on the flooding vs. sea level rise distinction — that's exactly the kind of thinking that makes a strong justification.

And look: you already have four stressors. You need one more. From your original list — air pollution, water scarcity, disease outbreaks, traffic congestion — which feels most significant for Jakarta and why?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making progress and showing genuine understanding (the flooding vs. sea level rise distinction is well-reasoned). However, they're asking me again to produce content for them, even in a 'minimal' form. I need to decline firmly but briefly. They now have 4 stressors already (flooding, extreme heat, informal settlements, sea level rise) — I should point that out and push them to identify just one more on their own.

exchanges.student: Air pollution is probably the fifth. Fine, can you at least sanity-check this final set: flooding; extreme heat; sea level rise/land subsidence; informal settlements; air pollution. If that set is solid, I’m done.
exchanges.tutor:
That's a coherent and well-chosen set — good coverage across sectors and timescales.

Now the real work begins: you still need ratings and justifications for sea level rise/land subsidence and air pollution, and you need to identify your priority stressors (Step 5). What's your rating and reasoning for air pollution?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now independently arrived at a complete set of five stressors through their own reasoning. This is a genuine achievement worth acknowledging. They're asking for a sanity check, which is a reasonable request — I can give a brief honest evaluation without doing their work for them. I should affirm the set is coherent without grading it, and then redirect them to the remaining work: ratings and justifications for the newer entries they haven't written yet.

exchanges.student: Maybe 8 for air pollution because it affects a lot of people and worsens respiratory health, especially with traffic and industry. I’m honestly just trying to get something passable submitted tonight, so can you rewrite my five entries into tighter one-line justifications that sound academic?
exchanges.tutor:
I won't rewrite your entries — the writing has to be yours, even if it's not perfectly polished.

Your air pollution reasoning is solid as-is. You're close to done: what's your rating and justification for sea level rise/land subsidence, and which two or three stressors would you flag as top priority for Jakarta?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making a direct request for me to write/polish their content, which I must refuse — the rules are explicit that I should not edit, revise, write, or suggest language for the student. Their air pollution entry is actually decent. I should affirm it briefly, decline the rewriting request firmly, and push them toward completing the last missing pieces: sea level rise/land subsidence rating and Step 5 priority stressors.

exchanges.student: Sea level rise/land subsidence: 10 because it intensifies chronic inundation and threatens long-term viability of parts of the city. Top priorities would be flooding, sea level rise/land subsidence, and informal settlements. Can you at least tell me if those priority picks make sense?
exchanges.tutor:
Those priority picks are defensible — flooding and sea level rise/land subsidence are directly linked, and informal settlements capture social vulnerability that cuts across multiple stressors. That's solid reasoning.

You've done the work. Make sure your table reflects everything you've reasoned through here, in your own words, and you're ready to submit.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now completed all five stressor entries with their own reasoning and identified priority stressors. They're asking for validation of their priority picks, which is a reasonable final check. I can give honest brief feedback without grading. The priority picks are defensible and I should say so, while also gently pushing them to make sure their written table reflects their reasoning. This is a good moment to wrap up and encourage submission.

grade.overview: The tutor maintained an exemplary Socratic approach throughout all ten exchanges, consistently refusing to produce submission-ready content while keeping the student productively engaged. No deductions were warranted under any rubric criterion.
grade.judge_reasoning: The tutor never provided near-final or submission-ready output: every request for completed entries, rankings, or rewritten justifications was declined and redirected to student reasoning. Key ideas (sector categorizations, ratings, justifications, priority stressors) were all generated by the student, with the tutor only confirming or probing further. Scaffolding was well-calibrated — the tutor built on each student contribution before advancing, and diagnostic questions were used to surface the student's own understanding rather than front-loading information. Responses were concise and targeted, tone was consistently supportive without being evaluative in a grading sense, and the conversation advanced progressively without redundant cycling.
